title: 50 Cent

infobox.value: Curtis James Jackson III   (1975-07-06) July 6, 1975 (age 50)  New York City, U.S.
* Rapper * songwriter * actor * television producer * record executive * businessman

* [Albums](/wiki/50_Cent_albums_discography "50 Cent albums discography") * [singles](/wiki/50_Cent_singles_discography "50 Cent singles discography") * [group](/wiki/G-Unit_discography "G-Unit discography")   + [records](/wiki/G-Unit_Records "G-Unit Records")   + [clothing](/wiki/G-Unit_Clothing_Company "G-Unit Clothing Company")   + [books](/wiki/G-Unit_Books "G-Unit Books")   + [multimedia](/wiki/G-Unit_Films_and_Television_Inc. "G-Unit Films and Television Inc.") * [videography](/wiki/50_Cent_videography "50 Cent videography")

* *[50 Central](/wiki/50_Central "50 Central")* * *[50 Cent: The Money and the Power](/wiki/50_Cent:_The_Money_and_the_Power "50 Cent: The Money and the Power")* * *[For Life](/wiki/For_Life_(TV_series) "For Life (TV series)")* * *[Power](/wiki/Power_(TV_series) "Power (TV series)")* * *[The Oath](/wiki/The_Oath_(U.S._TV_series) "The Oath (U.S. TV series)")*[[2]](#cite_note-2)
[Full list](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_50_Cent "List of awards and nominations received by 50 Cent")

* [Caroline](/wiki/Caroline_Distribution "Caroline Distribution") * [Capitol](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records") * [G-Unit](/wiki/G-Unit_Records "G-Unit Records") * [Shady](/wiki/Shady_Records "Shady Records") * [Aftermath](/wiki/Aftermath_Entertainment "Aftermath Entertainment") * [Interscope](/wiki/Interscope_Records "Interscope Records") * [Universal](/wiki/Universal_Music_Group "Universal Music Group") * [Columbia](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records") * [Trackmasters](/wiki/Trackmasters "Trackmasters") * [JMJ](/wiki/JMJ_Records "JMJ Records")

(born July 6, 1975),[[3]](#cite_note-allmusicbio-3) known professionally as **50 Cent**,[[n 1]](#cite_note-5) is an American rapper, actor, television producer, record executive, and businessman. Born in [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), a [borough](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City") of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), Jackson began pursuing a musical career in 1996. In 1999–2000, he recorded his debut album, *[Power of the Dollar](/wiki/Power_of_the_Dollar "Power of the Dollar")*, for [Columbia Records](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records"). During a shooting in May 2000, he was struck by nine bullets, causing its release to be canceled and Jackson to be dropped from the label. His 2002 mixtape *[Guess Who's Back?](/wiki/Guess_Who%27s_Back%3F "Guess Who's Back?")* was discovered by Detroit rapper [Eminem](/wiki/Eminem "Eminem"), who signed Jackson to his label [Shady Records](/wiki/Shady_Records "Shady Records") (an imprint of [Interscope Records](/wiki/Interscope_Records "Interscope Records")) that year.[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-7)

**Curtis James Jackson III** (born July 6, 1975),[[3]](#cite_note-allmusicbio-3) known professionally as **50 Cent**,[[n 1]](#cite_note-5) is an American rapper, actor, television producer, record executive, and businessman. Born in [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), a [borough](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City") of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), Jackson began pursuing a musical career in 1996. In 1999–2000, he recorded his debut album, *[Power of the Dollar](/wiki/Power_of_the_Dollar "Power of the Dollar")*, for [Columbia Records](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records"). During a shooting in May 2000, he was struck by nine bullets, causing its release to be canceled and Jackson to be dropped from the label. His 2002 mixtape *[Guess Who's Back?](/wiki/Guess_Who%27s_Back%3F "Guess Who's Back?")* was discovered by Detroit rapper [Eminem](/wiki/Eminem "Eminem"), who signed Jackson to his label [Shady Records](/wiki/Shady_Records "Shady Records") (an imprint of [Interscope Records](/wiki/Interscope_Records "Interscope Records")) that year.[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-7)

Jackson's debut studio album, *[Get Rich or Die Tryin'](/wiki/Get_Rich_or_Die_Tryin%27 "Get Rich or Die Tryin'")* (2003), was released to critical acclaim and commercial success. Peaking atop the [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200"), it spawned the [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100")-number one singles "[In da Club](/wiki/In_da_Club "In da Club")" and "[21 Questions](/wiki/21_Questions "21 Questions")" (featuring [Nate Dogg](/wiki/Nate_Dogg "Nate Dogg")), and received [Multiple Platinum](/wiki/RIAA_certification "RIAA certification") certification by the [Recording Industry Association of America](/wiki/Recording_Industry_Association_of_America "Recording Industry Association of America") (RIAA). Also in 2003, he launched the record label [G-Unit Records](/wiki/G-Unit_Records "G-Unit Records"), namesake of [a hip-hop group](/wiki/G-Unit "G-Unit") he formed two years earlier; the label's initial signees were its members, fellow East Coast rappers [Lloyd Banks](/wiki/Lloyd_Banks "Lloyd Banks") and [Tony Yayo](/wiki/Tony_Yayo "Tony Yayo"). His second album, *[The Massacre](/wiki/The_Massacre "The Massacre")* (2005), met with continued success, yielding his third number-one single, "[Candy Shop](/wiki/Candy_Shop "Candy Shop")" (featuring [Olivia](/wiki/Olivia_(singer) "Olivia (singer)")). He took a lighter, more commercially oriented approach with his third and fourth albums, *[Curtis](/wiki/Curtis_(50_Cent_album) "Curtis (50 Cent album)")* (2007) and *[Before I Self Destruct](/wiki/Before_I_Self_Destruct "Before I Self Destruct")* (2009)—both of which witnessed critical and commercial declines—and aimed for a return to his roots with his fifth album, *[Animal Ambition](/wiki/Animal_Ambition "Animal Ambition")* (2014), which received mixed reviews. He has since focused on television and media, having executive-produced and starred in the television series *[Power](/wiki/Power_(TV_series) "Power (TV series)")* (2014–2020), as well as its numerous spin-offs under his company [G-Unit Films and Television Inc.](/wiki/G-Unit_Films_and_Television_Inc. "G-Unit Films and Television Inc.")[[7]](#cite_note-8)

Jackson has sold over 30 million albums worldwide and earned [several accolades](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_50_Cent "List of awards and nominations received by 50 Cent"), including a [Grammy Award](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards"), a [Primetime Emmy Award](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Awards "Primetime Emmy Awards"), 13 [*Billboard* Music Awards](/wiki/Billboard_Music_Awards "Billboard Music Awards"), six [World Music Awards](/wiki/World_Music_Awards "World Music Awards"), three [American Music Awards](/wiki/American_Music_Awards "American Music Awards"), and four [BET Awards](/wiki/BET_Awards "BET Awards").[[8]](#cite_note-9) He starred in the semi-autobiographical film *[Get Rich or Die Tryin'](/wiki/Get_Rich_or_Die_Tryin%27_(film) "Get Rich or Die Tryin' (film)")* (2005), which was critically panned. He also appeared in the war film *[Home of the Brave](/wiki/Home_of_the_Brave_(2006_film) "Home of the Brave (2006 film)")* (2006) and the crime thriller *[Righteous Kill](/wiki/Righteous_Kill "Righteous Kill")* (2008). *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* ranked Jackson 17th on its "50 Greatest Rappers" list in 2023,[[9]](#cite_note-10) and named him the sixth top artist of the 2000s decade.[[10]](#cite_note-11) *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* ranked *Get Rich or Die Tryin'* and "In da Club" in its lists of the "100 Best Albums of the 2000s" and "100 Best Songs of the 2000s" at numbers 37 and 13, respectively.[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13)

## Early life

Jackson was born in [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), New York City, and raised in the [South Jamaica](/wiki/South_Jamaica,_Queens "South Jamaica, Queens") neighborhood[[3]](#cite_note-allmusicbio-3) by his mother, Sabrina. A drug dealer, Sabrina raised Jackson until she died in a fire when Jackson was eight years old.[[13]](#cite_note-MSNBC-14)[[14]](#cite_note-RS-15) Jackson said in an interview that she was a lesbian.[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17) After his mother's death and his father's departure, Jackson was raised by his grandparents.[[17]](#cite_note-OMM-18)

Jackson began boxing at about age 11, and when he was 14, a neighbor opened a boxing gym for local youth. "When I wasn't killing time in school, I was sparring in the gym or selling [crack](/wiki/Crack_cocaine "Crack cocaine") on the strip", he has said.[[18]](#cite_note-19) He sold crack during primary school.[[19]](#cite_note-20) "I was competitive in the ring and hip-hop is competitive too ... I think rappers condition themselves like boxers, so they all kind of feel like they're the champ."[[20]](#cite_note-MTV2-21)

At age 12, Jackson began dealing [narcotics](/wiki/Narcotic "Narcotic") when his grandparents thought he was in after-school programs,[[21]](#cite_note-22) and brought guns and drug money to school. In tenth grade, he was caught by [metal detectors](/wiki/Metal_detector "Metal detector") at [Andrew Jackson High School](/wiki/Andrew_Jackson_High_School_(Queens) "Andrew Jackson High School (Queens)"): "I was embarrassed that I got arrested like that ... After I got arrested I stopped hiding it. I was telling my grandmother [openly], 'I sell drugs.'"[[22]](#cite_note-MTV-23)

On June 29, 1994, Jackson was arrested for selling four vials of cocaine to an undercover police officer. He was arrested again three weeks later when police searched his home and found heroin, ten ounces of crack cocaine, and a [starting pistol](/wiki/Starting_pistol "Starting pistol"). Jackson was sentenced to three to nine years in prison, but served six months in a [boot camp](/wiki/Boot_camp_(correctional)#United_States "Boot camp (correctional)") and earned his [GED](/wiki/General_Educational_Development "General Educational Development"). He has said that he did not use cocaine himself.[[17]](#cite_note-OMM-18)[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-rapnewsdirect.com-25) Jackson adopted the nickname "50 Cent" as a [metaphor](/wiki/Metaphor "Metaphor") for change.[[25]](#cite_note-AOL-26) The name was inspired by [Kelvin Martin](/wiki/Kelvin_Martin "Kelvin Martin"), a 1980s Brooklyn robber known as "50 Cent"; Jackson chose it "because it says everything I want it to say. I'm the same kind of person 50 Cent was. I provide for myself by any means."[[26]](#cite_note-27)

## Career

### 1996–2002: Rise to fame, shooting, and early mixtapes

Jackson began rapping in a friend's basement, using [turntables](/wiki/Phonograph "Phonograph") to record over instrumentals.[[27]](#cite_note-28) In 1996, a friend introduced him to [Jam Master Jay](/wiki/Jam_Master_Jay "Jam Master Jay") of [Run-DMC](/wiki/Run%E2%80%93D.M.C. "Run–D.M.C."), who was establishing [Jam Master Jay Records](/wiki/Jam_Master_Jay_Records "Jam Master Jay Records"). Jay taught him to count [bars](/wiki/Bar_(music) "Bar (music)"), write [choruses](/wiki/Refrain "Refrain"), structure songs, and make records.[[28]](#cite_note-BBC-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30) In 1997, A&R of Def Jam [Irv Gotti](/wiki/Irv_Gotti "Irv Gotti") turned down demo tapes by Jackson that Jay showed him for a potential record deal on the grounds that they were too similar to Jay-Z.[[30]](#cite_note-z144-31) Jackson's first appearance was on "[React](/wiki/React_(Onyx_song) "React (Onyx song)")" with [Onyx](/wiki/Onyx_(hip-hop_group) "Onyx (hip-hop group)"), from its 1998 album *[Shut 'Em Down](/wiki/Shut_%27Em_Down_(album) "Shut 'Em Down (album)")*, which Gotti was the A&R for. He credited Jam Master Jay for improving his ability to write [hooks](/wiki/Hook_(music) "Hook (music)"),[[20]](#cite_note-MTV2-21) and Jay produced Jackson's first (unreleased) album.[[14]](#cite_note-RS-15) In 1999, after Jackson left Jam Master Jay, the platinum-selling producers [Trackmasters](/wiki/Trackmasters "Trackmasters") signed him to [Columbia Records](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records"). They sent him to an [upstate New York](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York") studio, where he produced 36 songs in two weeks;[[13]](#cite_note-MSNBC-14) 18 were included on his 2000 album *[Power of the Dollar](/wiki/Power_of_the_Dollar "Power of the Dollar")*.[[31]](#cite_note-50Cent-32) Jackson founded Hollow Point Entertainment with former [G-Unit](/wiki/G-Unit "G-Unit") member Bang 'Em Smurf.[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34)

Jackson's popularity began to grow after the successful, controversial underground single "[How to Rob](/wiki/How_to_Rob "How to Rob")", which he wrote in a half-hour car ride to a studio.[[25]](#cite_note-AOL-26)[[34]](#cite_note-Part5-35) The track comically describes how he would rob famous artists. Jackson explained the song's rationale: "There's a hundred artists on that label, you gotta separate yourself from that group and make yourself relevant."[[25]](#cite_note-AOL-26) Rappers [Jay-Z](/wiki/Jay-Z "Jay-Z"), [Kurupt](/wiki/Kurupt "Kurupt"), [Sticky Fingaz](/wiki/Sticky_Fingaz "Sticky Fingaz"), [Big Pun](/wiki/Big_Pun "Big Pun"), [Wyclef Jean](/wiki/Wyclef_Jean "Wyclef Jean"), and the [Wu-Tang Clan](/wiki/Wu-Tang_Clan "Wu-Tang Clan") responded to the track,[[34]](#cite_note-Part5-35) and [Nas](/wiki/Nas "Nas") invited Jackson to join him on his *[Nastradamus](/wiki/Nastradamus "Nastradamus")* tour.[[35]](#cite_note-MTV3-36) Although "How to Rob" was intended to be released with "[Thug Love](/wiki/Thug_Love_(song) "Thug Love (song)")" (with [Destiny's Child](/wiki/Destiny%27s_Child "Destiny's Child")), two days before he was scheduled to film the "Thug Love" video, Jackson was shot and hospitalized.[[36]](#cite_note-dubcnn-37)

On May 24, 2000, Jackson was attacked by a gunman outside his grandmother's former home in South Jamaica. After getting into a friend's car, he was asked to return to the house to get some jewelry; his son was in the house, and his grandmother was in the front yard.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Jackson returned to the car, and another car pulled up nearby; an assailant walked up and fired nine shots at close range with a [9mm](/wiki/9_mm_caliber "9 mm caliber") [handgun](/wiki/Handgun "Handgun"). Jackson was shot in the hand, arm, hip, both legs, chest, and left cheek.[[14]](#cite_note-RS-15)[[22]](#cite_note-MTV-23)[[37]](#cite_note-TIME-38) His facial wound resulted in a swollen tongue, the loss of a wisdom tooth and a slightly slurred voice;[[22]](#cite_note-MTV-23)[[35]](#cite_note-MTV3-36)[[38]](#cite_note-39) his friend was wounded in the hand. They were driven to a hospital, where Jackson spent 13 days. The alleged attacker, Darryl "Homicide" Baum, [Mike Tyson](/wiki/Mike_Tyson "Mike Tyson")'s close friend and bodyguard,[[39]](#cite_note-40) was killed three weeks later.[[40]](#cite_note-SOHH-41)

Jackson recalled the shooting: "It happens so fast that you don't even get a chance to shoot back .... I was scared the whole time ... I was looking in the rear-view mirror like, 'Oh shit, somebody shot me in the face! It burns, burns, burns.'"[[22]](#cite_note-MTV-23) In his autobiography, *From Pieces to Weight: Once upon a Time in Southside Queens*, he wrote: "After I got shot nine times at close range and didn't die, I started to think that I must have a purpose in life ... How much more damage could that shell have done? Give me an inch in this direction or that one, and I'm gone."[[17]](#cite_note-OMM-18) Jackson used a [walker](/wiki/Walker_(mobility) "Walker (mobility)") for six weeks and fully recovered after five months. When he left the hospital, he stayed in the [Poconos](/wiki/Pocono_Mountains "Pocono Mountains") with his girlfriend and son, and his workout regime helped him develop a muscular physique.[[14]](#cite_note-RS-15)[[22]](#cite_note-MTV-23)[[41]](#cite_note-42)

In the hospital, Jackson signed a publishing deal with Columbia Records before he was dropped from the label and blacklisted by the recording industry because of his song "[Ghetto Qu'ran](/wiki/Ghetto_Qu%27ran_(Forgive_Me) "Ghetto Qu'ran (Forgive Me)")". Unable to work in a U.S. studio, he went to Canada.[[42]](#cite_note-TSG-43)[[43]](#cite_note-Blender-44) With business partner [Sha Money XL](/wiki/Sha_Money_XL "Sha Money XL"), Jackson recorded over 30 songs for [mixtapes](/wiki/Mixtape "Mixtape") to build a reputation. In a [HitQuarters](/wiki/HitQuarters "HitQuarters") interview, Marc Labelle of [Shady Records](/wiki/Shady_Records "Shady Records") [A&R](/wiki/Artists_and_repertoire "Artists and repertoire") said Jackson used the mixtape circuit to his advantage: "He took all the hottest beats from every artist and flipped them with better hooks. They then got into all the markets on the mixtapes and all the mixtape DJs were messing with them."[[44]](#cite_note-hitquarters.com-45) Jackson's popularity increased, and in 2002 he released the mixtape *[Guess Who's Back?](/wiki/Guess_Who%27s_Back%3F "Guess Who's Back?")*. He then released *[50 Cent Is the Future](/wiki/50_Cent_Is_the_Future "50 Cent Is the Future")* backed by [G-Unit](/wiki/G-Unit "G-Unit"), a mixtape revisiting material by [Jay-Z](/wiki/Jay-Z "Jay-Z") and [Raphael Saadiq](/wiki/Raphael_Saadiq "Raphael Saadiq").[[31]](#cite_note-50Cent-32)

### 2002–2007: Mainstream breakthrough, *Get Rich or Die Tryin*, and *The Massacre*

> "One of the things that excited me about [Tupac](/wiki/Tupac "Tupac") was even if he was rhymin' the simplest words in the world, you felt like he meant it and it came from his heart. That's the thing with 50. That same aura. That's been missing since we lost Pac and [Biggie](/wiki/Biggie "Biggie"). The authenticity, the realness behind it."

—Eminem about signing 50 Cent[[45]](#cite_note-46)

In 2002, [Eminem](/wiki/Eminem "Eminem") received *[Guess Who's Back?](/wiki/Guess_Who%27s_Back%3F "Guess Who's Back?")* from Jackson's attorney, who was working with Eminem's manager, [Paul Rosenberg](/wiki/Paul_Rosenberg_(music_manager) "Paul Rosenberg (music manager)").[[36]](#cite_note-dubcnn-37) Impressed, Eminem invited Jackson to [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") and introduced him to [Dr. Dre](/wiki/Dr._Dre "Dr. Dre").[[14]](#cite_note-RS-15)[[28]](#cite_note-BBC-29)[[36]](#cite_note-dubcnn-37) After signing a $1 million record deal,[[28]](#cite_note-BBC-29) Jackson released the mixtape *[No Mercy, No Fear](/wiki/No_Mercy,_No_Fear "No Mercy, No Fear")*. It featured one new track, "[Wanksta](/wiki/Wanksta "Wanksta")", which appeared on Eminem's *[8 Mile](/wiki/Music_from_and_Inspired_by_the_Motion_Picture_8_Mile "Music from and Inspired by the Motion Picture 8 Mile")* soundtrack.[[31]](#cite_note-50Cent-32) Jackson was also signed by [Violator Management](/wiki/Violator_(company) "Violator (company)") and [Sha Money XL](/wiki/Sha_Money_XL "Sha Money XL")'s Money Management Group.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Jackson released his debut album, *[Get Rich or Die Tryin'](/wiki/Get_Rich_or_Die_Tryin%27 "Get Rich or Die Tryin'")* (described by [AllMusic](/wiki/AllMusic "AllMusic") as "probably the most hyped debut album by a rap artist in about a decade"), in February 2003.[[46]](#cite_note-47) *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* noted its "dark synth grooves, buzzy keyboards and a persistently funky bounce", with Jackson complementing the production in "an unflappable, laid-back flow".[[47]](#cite_note-48) It debuted at number one on the [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200"), selling 872,000 copies in its first four days.[[48]](#cite_note-USA-49) The lead single, "[In da Club](/wiki/In_da_Club "In da Club")" (noted by *[The Source](/wiki/The_Source_(magazine) "The Source (magazine)")* for its "blaring horns, funky organs, guitar riffs and sparse hand claps"),[[49]](#cite_note-50) set a *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* record as the most listened-to song in radio history within a week.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/50_Cent_%28Cropped%29.jpg/250px-50_Cent_%28Cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:50_Cent_(Cropped).jpg)

50 Cent in 2006

[Interscope](/wiki/Interscope_Records "Interscope Records") began funding and distributing for Jackson's label, [G-Unit Records](/wiki/G-Unit_Records "G-Unit Records"), in 2003.[[51]](#cite_note-52) He signed [Lloyd Banks](/wiki/Lloyd_Banks "Lloyd Banks"), [Tony Yayo](/wiki/Tony_Yayo "Tony Yayo"), and [Young Buck](/wiki/Young_Buck "Young Buck") as members of [G-Unit](/wiki/G-Unit "G-Unit"), and [The Game](/wiki/The_Game_(rapper) "The Game (rapper)") was later signed in a [joint venture](/wiki/Joint_venture "Joint venture") with Dr. Dre's [Aftermath Entertainment](/wiki/Aftermath_Entertainment "Aftermath Entertainment"). G-Unit Records replaced Jackson's previous imprint, Rotten Apple Entertainment.[[52]](#cite_note-53) 50 Cent executive produced Lloyd Banks's 2004 debut studio album, *[The Hunger for More](/wiki/The_Hunger_for_More "The Hunger for More")*, which achieved platinum status in the U.S. Jackson also contributed vocals to Banks's hit single "[On Fire](/wiki/On_Fire_(Lloyd_Banks_song) "On Fire (Lloyd Banks song)")". In March 2005, Jackson's second commercial album, *[The Massacre](/wiki/The_Massacre "The Massacre")*, sold 1.14 million copies in its first four days (the highest in an abbreviated sales cycle[[48]](#cite_note-USA-49)) and was number one on the *Billboard* 200 for six weeks.[[53]](#cite_note-54) He was the first solo artist with three singles in the *Billboard* top five in the same week with "[Candy Shop](/wiki/Candy_Shop "Candy Shop")", "[Disco Inferno](/wiki/Disco_Inferno_(50_Cent_song) "Disco Inferno (50 Cent song)")" and "[How We Do](/wiki/How_We_Do_(song) "How We Do (song)")".[[54]](#cite_note-55) According to *Rolling Stone*, "50's secret weapon is his singing voice—the deceptively amateur-sounding tenor croon that he deploys on almost every chorus".[[55]](#cite_note-56) Jackson's video game, *[50 Cent: Bulletproof](/wiki/50_Cent:_Bulletproof "50 Cent: Bulletproof")* was released in November 2005. He portrays himself and provides his likeness and voice in the game, which features music from his first two studio albums.

[![Three men and a woman holding decorative elephants](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/GUnitBKK.JPG/250px-GUnitBKK.JPG)](/wiki/File:GUnitBKK.JPG)

[Olivia](/wiki/Olivia_(singer) "Olivia (singer)"), [Lloyd Banks](/wiki/Lloyd_Banks "Lloyd Banks"), [Young Buck](/wiki/Young_Buck "Young Buck"), and 50 Cent (left to right) in Bangkok, February 2006

After The Game's departure, Jackson signed [Olivia](/wiki/Olivia_(singer) "Olivia (singer)") and rap veterans [Mobb Deep](/wiki/Mobb_Deep "Mobb Deep") to G-Unit Records, and later [Spider Loc](/wiki/Spider_Loc "Spider Loc"), [M.O.P.](/wiki/M.O.P. "M.O.P."), [40 Glocc](/wiki/40_Glocc "40 Glocc"), and Young Hot Rod. All eventually left the label.[[56]](#cite_note-57)[[57]](#cite_note-58) Jackson expressed interest in working with rappers other than G-Unit, such as [Lil' Scrappy](/wiki/Lil_Scrappy "Lil Scrappy") of [BME](/wiki/BME_Recordings "BME Recordings"), [LL Cool J](/wiki/LL_Cool_J "LL Cool J") of [Def Jam](/wiki/Def_Jam_Recordings "Def Jam Recordings"), [Mase](/wiki/Mase "Mase") of [Bad Boy](/wiki/Bad_Boy_Records "Bad Boy Records"), and [Freeway](/wiki/Freeway_(rapper) "Freeway (rapper)") of [Roc-A-Fella](/wiki/Roc-A-Fella_Records "Roc-A-Fella Records"), and recorded with several.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

### 2007–2010: *Curtis*, sales battle with Kanye West, and *Before I Self Destruct*

In September 2007, Jackson released his third album, *[Curtis](/wiki/Curtis_(50_Cent_album) "Curtis (50 Cent album)")*, inspired by his life before *Get Rich or Die Tryin'*.[[59]](#cite_note-60) It debuted at number two on the *Billboard* 200, selling 691,000 copies during its first week.[[60]](#cite_note-61) It sold behind [Kanye West](/wiki/Kanye_West "Kanye West")'s *[Graduation](/wiki/Graduation_(album) "Graduation (album)")*, released the same day; the outcome of this highly publicized sales battle between Jackson and West has been accredited to the commercial decline of the [gangsta rap](/wiki/Gangsta_rap "Gangsta rap") and "bling era" style that previously dominated mainstream hip-hop.[[61]](#cite_note-Burney-62)

In 2008, 50 Cent co-signed the underground rap group Bang Bang Boogie, which consisted of Mysonne, [Cuban Link](/wiki/Cuban_Link "Cuban Link"), Lord Tariq, Hocus 45th and S-One, giving them his stamp of approval. The group released the mixtapes *The Machine, Vol. 1* and *X-Files: No Mercy For The Weak* in that same year before disbanding shortly due to Hocus and S-One being falsely incarcerated for gang-related crimes. The group was known for representing [The Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx") and for their beef with [Fat Joe](/wiki/Fat_Joe "Fat Joe") as they dissed him on songs such as "High Blood Pressure" and "Bang Out".[[62]](#cite_note-63)

On September 10, 2008, episode of *[Total Request Live](/wiki/Total_Request_Live "Total Request Live"),* Jackson said his fourth studio album, *[Before I Self Destruct](/wiki/Before_I_Self_Destruct "Before I Self Destruct")*, would be "done and released in November". He released "[Ok, You're Right](/wiki/Ok,_You%27re_Right "Ok, You're Right")", produced by Dr. Dre for *[Before I Self Destruct](/wiki/Before_I_Self_Destruct "Before I Self Destruct")*, on May 18, 2009, and was scheduled to appear in a fall 2009 episode of [VH1](/wiki/VH1 "VH1")'s *[Behind the Music](/wiki/Behind_the_Music "Behind the Music")*. On September 3, 2009, Jackson posted a video[[63]](#cite_note-64) for the Soundkillers' Phoenix-[[64]](#cite_note-65) produced track, "Flight 187", introducing his mixtape and book (*[The 50th Law](/wiki/The_50th_Law "The 50th Law")*). The song, with lyrics inspiring speculation about tension between Jackson and Jay-Z, was a bonus track on the iTunes version of *Before I Self Destruct*.[[65]](#cite_note-66) *[Before I Self Destruct](/wiki/Before_I_Self_Destruct "Before I Self Destruct")* was released on November 9, 2009, and debuted at number 5 on the *Billboard 200*, giving 50 Cent his fourth consecutive top 5 album in the U.S.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

### 2010–2015: New musical directions, new business ventures, and *Animal Ambition*

In a [Contactmusic.com](/wiki/Contactmusic.com "Contactmusic.com") interview, Jackson said he was working on a [Eurodance](/wiki/Eurodance "Eurodance") album, *Black Magic*, inspired by European nightclubs: "First they played hip-hop which suddenly changed to uptempo songs, known as Eurodance".[[67]](#cite_note-68) He later said he had changed his next album to *The Return of the Heartless Monster* after writing different material when he returned home from [the Invitation Tour](/wiki/The_Invitation_Tour "The Invitation Tour") in 2010, shelving *Black Magic*.[[68]](#cite_note-69)[[69]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-70) On September 3, Jackson supported Eminem on his and [Jay-Z](/wiki/Jay-Z "Jay-Z")'s [The Home & Home Tour](/wiki/The_Home_%26_Home_Tour "The Home & Home Tour"), performing "Crack A Bottle" with Eminem and Dr. Dre amid rumors of tension between Jackson and Dre.[[70]](#cite_note-71)[[71]](#cite_note-72)

He "recorded 20 songs to a whole different album concept" before putting them aside,[[72]](#cite_note-73) wanting his new album to have the "aggression" of *Get Rich or Die Tryin'*.[[73]](#cite_note-mtv.co.uk-74)[[74]](#cite_note-75) Jackson tweeted that the album was "80 percent done" and fans could expect it in the summer of 2011. It was ultimately delayed a year due to disagreements with Interscope Records, with Jackson saying that he would release it in November 2011[[75]](#cite_note-hiphopdx1-76) with a different title than *Black Magic*.[[75]](#cite_note-hiphopdx1-76) Eminem would appear on the album, and Jackson said he was working with new producers such as [Boi-1da](/wiki/Boi-1da "Boi-1da") and [Alex da Kid](/wiki/Alex_da_Kid "Alex da Kid").[[76]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-77) [Cardiak](/wiki/Cardiak "Cardiak"), who produced [Lloyd Banks](/wiki/Lloyd_Banks "Lloyd Banks")'s "Start It Up", confirmed that he produced a song for the album.[[77]](#cite_note-78)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/50_Cent-4_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-50_Cent-4_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:50_Cent-4_(cropped).jpg)

50 Cent performing in 2011

Jackson released a song, "[Outlaw](/wiki/Outlaw_(50_Cent_song) "Outlaw (50 Cent song)")", from his fifth album online on June 16, 2011.[[78]](#cite_note-79) The single, produced by Cardiak, was released on [iTunes](/wiki/ITunes "ITunes") on July 19[[79]](#cite_note-80) (although Jackson tweeted that it was not the album's first single).[[80]](#cite_note-81) Jackson planned to write a semi-autobiographical young-adult novel about bullying, different from his previous books, which focused on his life and the rules of power. According to the book's publisher, the first-person novel (about a 13-year-old schoolyard bully "who finds redemption as he faces what he's done")[[81]](#cite_note-82) was scheduled for publication in January 2012.

In a series of [tweets](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter"), Jackson said that the delay of his fifth album was due to disagreements with Interscope Records,[[75]](#cite_note-hiphopdx1-76) later suggesting that it would be released in November 2011 with his headphone line ([SMS by 50](/wiki/SMS_Audio "SMS Audio")).[[75]](#cite_note-hiphopdx1-76) He speculated to MTV News about not renewing his five-album contract with Interscope: "I don't know ... It will all be clear in the negotiations following me turning this actual album in. And, of course, the performance and how they actually treat the work will determine whether you still want to stay in that position or not."[[82]](#cite_note-83)

On June 20, 2011, Jackson announced the release of *Before I Self Destruct II* after his fifth album.[[83]](#cite_note-hiphopdx.com-84) Although he planned to shoot a video for the album's lead single, "I'm On It", on June 26,[[84]](#cite_note-85) it was never filmed.[[85]](#cite_note-86) Jackson told Shade45, "I did four songs in Detroit with Eminem. I did two with Just Blaze, a Boi-1da joint, and I did something with Alex da Kid. We made two that are definite singles and the other two are the kinds of records that we been making, more aimed at my core audience, more aggressive, more of a different kind of energy to it."[[86]](#cite_note-87) He released "Street King Energy Track #7" in September 2011 to promote Street King, his charity-based energy drink.[[87]](#cite_note-88) An announcement that Jackson was shooting a video for "Girls Go Wild", the fifth-album lead single featuring [Jeremih](/wiki/Jeremih "Jeremih"), was made on September 28, 2011.[[88]](#cite_note-89)[[89]](#cite_note-90)

Jackson's fifth album, *[Street King Immortal](/wiki/Street_King_Immortal "Street King Immortal")*, was first scheduled for a summer 2012 release but postponed to November 13.[[90]](#cite_note-91)[[91]](#cite_note-92) Disagreements with Interscope Records about its release and promotion led to its temporary cancellation. Its first [promo single](/wiki/Promo_single "Promo single"), "[New Day](/wiki/New_Day_(50_Cent_song) "New Day (50 Cent song)")" with [Dr. Dre](/wiki/Dr._Dre "Dr. Dre") and [Alicia Keys](/wiki/Alicia_Keys "Alicia Keys"), was released on July 27. The song was produced by Dr. Dre, mixed by [Eminem](/wiki/Eminem "Eminem"), and written by 50 Cent, Alicia Keys, [Royce da 5'9"](/wiki/Royce_da_5%279%22 "Royce da 5'9\"") and Dr. Dre. A solo version by Keys was [leaked](/wiki/News_leak "News leak") by her husband, [Swizz Beatz](/wiki/Swizz_Beatz "Swizz Beatz"). "[My Life](/wiki/My_Life_(50_Cent_song) "My Life (50 Cent song)")", the album's second promo single (with Eminem and [Maroon 5](/wiki/Maroon_5 "Maroon 5") lead singer [Adam Levine](/wiki/Adam_Levine "Adam Levine")), was released on November 26, 2012.

In January 2014, Jackson said he planned to release *[Animal Ambition](/wiki/Animal_Ambition "Animal Ambition")* in the first quarter of the year, followed by *Street King Immortal*.[[92]](#cite_note-93)[[93]](#cite_note-94) On February 20, he left Shady Records, Aftermath Entertainment, and Interscope, signing with [Caroline](/wiki/Caroline_Distribution "Caroline Distribution") and [Capitol Music Group](/wiki/Capitol_Music_Group "Capitol Music Group").[[94]](#cite_note-95) According to Jackson, although he owed Interscope another album, he was released from his contract because of his friendship with Eminem and Dr. Dre: "I'm a special case and situation. It's also because of the leverage of having the strong relationships with Eminem and Dr. Dre. They don't want me to be uncomfortable. They value our friendship to the point that they would never want [to jeopardize] it over that little bit of money."[[95]](#cite_note-96)

That day, he announced that *Animal Ambition* would be released on June 3[[96]](#cite_note-97) and released its first track. The song, "Funeral", was released with a video on Forbes.com. Produced by Jake One, it is a continuation of "50 Bars" from a previous album; two more tracks were scheduled for release on March 18.[[97]](#cite_note-98) At [South by Southwest](/wiki/South_by_Southwest "South by Southwest"), Jackson performed "[Hold On](/wiki/Hold_On_(50_Cent_song) "Hold On (50 Cent song)")" from the new album.[[98]](#cite_note-99) That song and "Don't Worry 'Bout It" were released with accompanying videos on March 18.[[99]](#cite_note-100) According to Jackson, prosperity would be a theme of the album: "This project, I had to search for a concept, a really good concept, in my perspective, and that was prosperity. I outlined all the things that would be a part of prosperity, positive and negative [for *Animal Ambition*]."[[100]](#cite_note-101)

*Animal Ambition* debuted at number four on the U.S. [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200"), giving 50 Cent his fifth consecutive top-five album in the country, while also debuting at number one on *Billboard's* [Independent Albums](/wiki/Independent_Albums "Independent Albums") chart.[[101]](#cite_note-102)

### 2015–2021: *Street King Immortal*, bankruptcy, and departure from Interscope

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Dont_Be_Mike_With_50_Cent_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Dont_Be_Mike_With_50_Cent_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dont_Be_Mike_With_50_Cent_(cropped).jpg)

50 Cent in 2017

On May 14, 2015, Jackson said in an interview that the first single from *Street King Immortal* would be previewed on Memorial Day weekend and likely be released in June.[[102]](#cite_note-103) He released "Get Low" on May 20 as the intended first single from his sixth studio album, *Street King Immortal*. The song, produced by Remo the Hitmaker, features vocals from [2 Chainz](/wiki/2_Chainz "2 Chainz"), [T.I.](/wiki/T.I. "T.I."), and [Jeremih](/wiki/Jeremih "Jeremih").[[103]](#cite_note-104) He announced bankruptcy on July 13, 2015.[[104]](#cite_note-105)

On March 31, 2017, Interscope Records released 50 Cent's final album for the label, the greatest-hits album *[Best Of](/wiki/Best_Of_(50_Cent_album) "Best Of (50 Cent album)")*.

In 2019, 50 Cent was featured on English singer-songwriter [Ed Sheeran](/wiki/Ed_Sheeran "Ed Sheeran")'s fourth studio album, [No.6 Collaborations Project](/wiki/No.6_Collaborations_Project "No.6 Collaborations Project") with American rapper [Eminem](/wiki/Eminem "Eminem"), on "[Remember the Name](/wiki/Remember_the_Name_(Ed_Sheeran_song) "Remember the Name (Ed Sheeran song)")".

In 2020, Jackson stepped in as executive producer for late rapper [Pop Smoke](/wiki/Pop_Smoke "Pop Smoke")'s debut album, *[Shoot for the Stars, Aim for the Moon](/wiki/Shoot_for_the_Stars,_Aim_for_the_Moon "Shoot for the Stars, Aim for the Moon")*, having been one of Pop Smoke's biggest inspirations. The album was released on July 3, 2020, to nationwide commercial acclaim.[[105]](#cite_note-106) Jackson curated the album, desiring to finish it after Pop had died. He contacted many of the artists involved, and also features on one of the album tracks, "[The Woo](/wiki/The_Woo "The Woo")". The Woo also features vocals by [Roddy Ricch](/wiki/Roddy_Ricch "Roddy Ricch").[[106]](#cite_note-107)[[107]](#cite_note-108)

In 2020, it was reported that Jackson was producing two television series for [Starz](/wiki/Starz "Starz"), an anthology about hip-hop and a biographical drama about sports agent [Nicole Lynn](/wiki/Nicole_Lynn "Nicole Lynn").[[108]](#cite_note-109)

In 2021, he became one of the headliners of the music festival *Golden Sand* in [Riviera Maya](/wiki/Riviera_Maya "Riviera Maya").[[109]](#cite_note-110)

In a July 2021 interview with *[The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent "The Independent")*, 50 Cent said he had decided to shelve his *Street King Immortal* album after it spent a decade in [development hell](/wiki/Development_hell "Development hell") and planned to release a completely new project.[[110]](#cite_note-111)

### 2021–present: Focusing on acting and media production, Super Bowl LVI halftime show performance

In May 2021, Jackson moved to [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston"). This was thought to be for lower taxes, no income tax, the rapper scene, and other ventures such as writing new screenplays. Jackson, Horizon United Group, and Houston Independent School District began a partnership on a project to help high school students learn the business skills.[[111]](#cite_note-112) While living in Houston, Jackson worked on screenplays for new crime shows.[[112]](#cite_note-113)

In August 2021, he was confirmed to be starring in the film *[Expend4bles](/wiki/Expend4bles "Expend4bles")*; it was released on September 22, 2023.[[113]](#cite_note-114) The film was a critical and commercial failure, grossing $51 million against a $100 million production budget.[[114]](#cite_note-115)

In September 2021, Starz began airing [*BMF*](/wiki/BMF_(TV_series) "BMF (TV series)"), a biographical series based on true events, depicting two brothers in Detroit (Demetrius "Big Meech" and Terry "Southwest T" Flenory, co-founders of the [Black Mafia Family](/wiki/Black_Mafia_Family "Black Mafia Family")) who ran a drug trafficking and money laundering operation from the mid-1980s until 2005. Jackson is the executive producer of the show. He also performed the show's theme song, "Wish Me Luck", alongside [Charlie Wilson](/wiki/Charlie_Wilson_(singer) "Charlie Wilson (singer)"), [Moneybagg Yo](/wiki/Moneybagg_Yo "Moneybagg Yo"), and [Snoop Dogg](/wiki/Snoop_Dogg "Snoop Dogg").[[115]](#cite_note-116)

On February 13, 2022, 50 Cent was a surprise performer in the [Super Bowl LVI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVI "Super Bowl LVI") halftime show,[[116]](#cite_note-117) receiving a [Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Variety Special (Live)](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Variety_Special_(Live) "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Variety Special (Live)") in September for [the performance](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVI_halftime_show "Super Bowl LVI halftime show").[[117]](#cite_note-118)

From December 2024 to January 2025, Jackson embarked on his Las Vegas residency, *50 Cent: In da Club*, which had been announced in October.[[118]](#cite_note-119) He also announced plans to executive-produce *[Sean Combs: The Reckoning](/wiki/Sean_Combs:_The_Reckoning "Sean Combs: The Reckoning")* for [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix"), a documentary centered on the [sexual misconduct allegations](/wiki/Sean_Combs_sexual_misconduct_allegations "Sean Combs sexual misconduct allegations") against hip-hop entrepreneur [Sean Combs](/wiki/Sean_Combs "Sean Combs"). It was released in December 2025.[[119]](#cite_note-120) In January 2025, *[Den of Thieves 2: Pantera](/wiki/Den_of_Thieves_2:_Pantera "Den of Thieves 2: Pantera")* was released as a sequel to the [2018 film](/wiki/Den_of_Thieves_(film) "Den of Thieves (film)"). Jackson, who starred in the 2018 film, executive produced the sequel alongside former co-star [O'Shea Jackson Jr.](/wiki/O%27Shea_Jackson_Jr. "O'Shea Jackson Jr."), who reprised his role.[[120]](#cite_note-121)

## Artistry

Jackson cites [Boogie Down Productions](/wiki/Boogie_Down_Productions "Boogie Down Productions"), [Big Daddy Kane](/wiki/Big_Daddy_Kane "Big Daddy Kane"), [The Juice Crew](/wiki/Juice_Crew "Juice Crew"), [EPMD](/wiki/EPMD "EPMD") and [KRS-One](/wiki/KRS-One "KRS-One") as his rapping influences, while citing [LL Cool J](/wiki/LL_Cool_J "LL Cool J") as an inspiration behind his writing of "[21 Questions](/wiki/21_Questions "21 Questions")".[[121]](#cite_note-122)[[122]](#cite_note-123) He has also said he was influenced by [Nas](/wiki/Nas "Nas"), [Rakim](/wiki/Rakim "Rakim"), and [The Notorious B.I.G.](/wiki/The_Notorious_B.I.G. "The Notorious B.I.G.") while working on *[Animal Ambition](/wiki/Animal_Ambition "Animal Ambition")*.[[123]](#cite_note-124)

## Business ventures

Jackson has had a highly successful business career. He is financially invested in a highly diversified variety of industries. Jackson is now involved in artist and talent management, record, television, and film production, footwear, apparel, fragrances, liquor, video games, mobile apps, book publishing, headphones, along with health drinks and dietary supplements.[[124]](#cite_note-125)[[125]](#cite_note-126) His broad business and investment portfolio contains investments in a variety of sectors including real estate, financial market investments, mining, boxing promotion, vodka, fragrances, consumer electronics and fashion.[[126]](#cite_note-127)

He established his own record label [G-Unit Records](/wiki/G-Unit_Records "G-Unit Records") in 2003 following his mainstream success.[[127]](#cite_note-Records_Signs_with_Interscope-128) In November 2003, he signed a five-year deal with [Reebok](/wiki/Reebok "Reebok") to distribute a G-Unit Sneakers line for his [G-Unit Clothing Company](/wiki/G-Unit_Clothing_Company "G-Unit Clothing Company").[[128]](#cite_note-129)[[129]](#cite_note-130) In an interview, Jackson said his businesses had a habit of doing well as he saw all of his ventures both past and present as revolving around his alter ego.[[130]](#cite_note-131)[[131]](#cite_note-132)

Jackson has also started a book publishing imprint, [G-Unit Books](/wiki/G-Unit_Books "G-Unit Books") on January 4, 2007, at the [Time Warner Building](/wiki/Time_Warner_Center "Time Warner Center") in New York.[[132]](#cite_note-133) He has written a number of books including a memoir, *From Pieces To Weight* in 2005 which sold 73,000 copies in hardcover and 14,000 copies in paperback; a crime novel and a book with [Robert Greene](/wiki/Robert_Greene_(American_author) "Robert Greene (American author)") titled *[The 50th Law](/wiki/The_50th_Law "The 50th Law")*, an urban take on *[The 48 Laws of Power](/wiki/The_48_Laws_of_Power "The 48 Laws of Power")*.[[133]](#cite_note-Kamelia_Angelova_and_Hilary_Lewis-134) In November 2011, Jackson released *50 Cent's Playground*, a [young adult fiction](/wiki/Young_adult_fiction "Young adult fiction") novel about a bullied, violent boy and his gay mother.[[134]](#cite_note-135)

One of Jackson's first business ventures was a partnership with [Glacéau](/wiki/Energy_Brands "Energy Brands") to create an [enhanced water](/wiki/Enhanced_water "Enhanced water") drink called Formula 50. In October 2004, Jackson became a beverage investor when he was given a minority share in the company in exchange for becoming a spokesperson after learning that he was a fan of the beverage. The health conscious Jackson noted that he first learned of the product while at a gym in Los Angeles, and stated that "they do such a good job making water taste good". After becoming a minority shareholder and celebrity spokesperson, Jackson worked with the company to create a new grape flavored "Formula 50" variant of VitaminWater and mentioned the drinks in various songs and interviews. In 2007, [Coca-Cola](/wiki/Coca-Cola "Coca-Cola") purchased Glacéau for $4.1 billion and, according to *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")*, Jackson, a minority shareholder, earned $100 million from the deal after taxes.[[135]](#cite_note-136)

Though he no longer has an equity stake in the company, Jackson continues[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] to act as a spokesperson for VitaminWater, supporting the product including singing about it at the [BET Awards](/wiki/BET_Awards "BET Awards") and expressing his excitement that the company continues to allow his input on products.[[136]](#cite_note-USAToday-137) He joined [Right Guard](/wiki/Right_Guard "Right Guard") to introduce a [body spray](/wiki/Body_spray "Body spray") (Pure 50 RGX) and endorsed Magic Stick condoms,[[137]](#cite_note-138) planning to donate part of their proceeds to increasing HIV awareness.[[138]](#cite_note-NYPost-139) Jackson signed a multi-year deal with Steiner Sports to sell his memorabilia,[[139]](#cite_note-140) and announced plans for a dietary-supplement company in conjunction with his film *Spectacular Regret* in August 2007.[[140]](#cite_note-141)[[141]](#cite_note-142)

[![50 Cent, smiling during an interview](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Val_Kilmer_and_50_Cent.jpg/250px-Val_Kilmer_and_50_Cent.jpg)](/wiki/File:Val_Kilmer_and_50_Cent.jpg)

50 Cent with [Val Kilmer](/wiki/Val_Kilmer "Val Kilmer") at the 2009 [American Music Awards](/wiki/American_Music_Award "American Music Award")

Jackson has founded two film production companies: [G-Unit Films](/wiki/G-Unit_Films_and_Television_Inc. "G-Unit Films and Television Inc.") in 2003 and [Cheetah Vision](/wiki/Cheetah_Vision "Cheetah Vision") in 2008.[[142]](#cite_note-143)[[143]](#cite_note-144) Cheetah Vision produces low-budget action thrillers for foreign film markets across the world.[[133]](#cite_note-Kamelia_Angelova_and_Hilary_Lewis-134)[[144]](#cite_note-50_Cent's_Massive_Business_Empire-145) When G-Unit Films folded, he focused on Cheetah Vision and the company obtained $200 million in funding in 2010.[[145]](#cite_note-146)[[146]](#cite_note-147) In 2010, Jackson revived G-Unit Films, renaming it G-Unit Film and Television, Inc.[[147]](#cite_note-148) The company had joint ventures with [Will Packer](/wiki/Will_Packer "Will Packer")'s production company, Will Packer Productions, and [Universal Television](/wiki/Universal_Television "Universal Television"). In over 18 months, Jackson sold projects to six different television networks. Among them was the [Courtney A. Kemp](/wiki/Courtney_A._Kemp "Courtney A. Kemp")-created *[Power](/wiki/Power_(TV_series) "Power (TV series)")*, a crime drama in which he co-starred as Kanan Stark and served as an executive producer. *Power* debuted on [Starz](/wiki/Starz "Starz") on June 7, 2014, and ended on February 9, 2020, after six seasons.[[148]](#cite_note-149)[[149]](#cite_note-150)[[150]](#cite_note-151) He signed a two-year contract with representation from the [Agency for the Performing Arts](/wiki/Agency_for_the_Performing_Arts "Agency for the Performing Arts"). Ratings have been a success for Starz, with the second-season premiere being the highest with 1.43 million viewers.[[151]](#cite_note-152)[[152]](#cite_note-153)[[153]](#cite_note-154)[[154]](#cite_note-155) Jackson is also the executive producer of three of the series' spin-offs, *[Power Book II: Ghost](/wiki/Power_Book_II:_Ghost "Power Book II: Ghost")*, *[Power Book III: Raising Kanan](/wiki/Power_Book_III:_Raising_Kanan "Power Book III: Raising Kanan")* and *[Power Book IV: Force](/wiki/Power_Book_IV:_Force "Power Book IV: Force")*. Offices for G-Unit Film and Television (also known as G-Unit Studios) are headquartered in [Shreveport, Louisiana](/wiki/Shreveport,_Louisiana "Shreveport, Louisiana"), as part of a 45-year lease agreement with the state.[[155]](#cite_note-156)[[156]](#cite_note-157)

In 2002, Jackson filed an application with the [United States Patent and Trademark Office](/wiki/United_States_Patent_and_Trademark_Office "United States Patent and Trademark Office") to register the term "50 Cent" as a trademark for clothing, sound recordings, and live performances. The application was published in 2003, and registration issued in 2004.[[157]](#cite_note-158) He has since filed for additional trademark registrations.

In July 2011, Jackson launched a philanthropic initiative to provide food for one billion starving people in Africa by 2016, joining Pure Growth Partners to introduce [Street King](/wiki/Street_King_(drink) "Street King (drink)").[[158]](#cite_note-159) A portion of the proceeds from each Street King purchase would be used to provide a daily meal to an underprivileged child. The partnership coincides with Jackson's goal to feed a billion people in Africa during the next five years. "50 Cent and I share a common vision: to address the world's problems through smart and sustainable business models," said Chris Clarke, founder and CEO of Pure Growth Partners. "With the rampant starvation in Africa and hunger afflicting children worldwide, we need socially responsible businesses that affect real change now more than ever." Jackson said, "I'm inspired by Clarke's vision and innovative approaches to tackling serious issues. It's our mission with Street King to really change children's lives around the world."[[159]](#cite_note-160)[[160]](#cite_note-161) In 2011, he founded [SMS Audio](/wiki/SMS_Audio "SMS Audio"), a consumer-electronics company selling Street by 50 headphones, pledging to donate a portion of their sales to charity.[[161]](#cite_note-162) In April 2015, SMS announced new co-branding deals with Reebok and Marvel. It added those to existing partnerships with Walt Disney Parks, Lucasfilm's Star Wars, and Intel.[[162]](#cite_note-163)[[163]](#cite_note-164)[[164]](#cite_note-165)

In 2014, Jackson became a minority shareholder in Effen Vodka, a brand of vodka produced in the Netherlands, when he invested undisclosed amount in the company, Sire Spirits, LLC.[[165]](#cite_note-166)[[166]](#cite_note-independent1-167) He endorsed the product via his live concert performances and social media. The rapper was asked to take part in two promotional bottle signings, one in Oak Creek and another in Sun Prairie. Jackson made an appearance at Liquor Warehouse in Syracuse, New York on April 25, 2015, where he reportedly sold 1,400 bottles (277 gallons) of Jackson's signature liquor brand. Liquor Warehouse's owner, George Angeloro, reportedly stocked 300 cases (1,800 bottles or 357 gallons) of Effen Vodka, which sells for $30 a bottle, prior to the event.[[167]](#cite_note-168)[[168]](#cite_note-169)

In December 2014, Jackson signed a $78 million deal with FRIGO Revolution Wear, a luxury underwear brand. The joint venture is partnered between Jackson, basketball player [Carmelo Anthony](/wiki/Carmelo_Anthony "Carmelo Anthony"), baseball player [Derek Jeter](/wiki/Derek_Jeter "Derek Jeter") and Mathias Ingvarsson, the former president of mattress company [Tempur-Pedic](/wiki/Tempur-Pedic "Tempur-Pedic"). Jackson became the chief fashion designer for the brands single pair of Frigo boxers.[[169]](#cite_note-170)[[170]](#cite_note-171) In April 2015, Jackson mulled investing in Jamaica, exploring foreign investment opportunities on the island when he met with some local officials and had ongoing discussions on investment opportunities in the Montego Bay resort area.[[171]](#cite_note-172)

### Investments

Over the years, Jackson invested his earnings from music and celebrity endorsements in an array of privately controlled companies, real estate, and stocks and bonds.[[133]](#cite_note-Kamelia_Angelova_and_Hilary_Lewis-134) A portion of his investments lost value during the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis").[[144]](#cite_note-50_Cent's_Massive_Business_Empire-145) In December 2008, he told the Canadian press that he had been affected by the recession, losing several million dollars in the stock market. Unable to sell his Connecticut mansion, Jackson postponed *Before I Self-Destruct* due to the severity of the economic downturn.[[172]](#cite_note-173)

His Farmington mansion at 50 Poplar Hill Drive that he tried to sell for years filed for bankruptcy in Connecticut in 2015. Its asking price in 2012 was $10 million but it was valued at $8.3 million in 2015. He first tried to sell the house in 2007 for $18.5 million, and dropped the price several times in the next five years, when it was on and off the market.[[173]](#cite_note-174)

In January 2011, Jackson reportedly made $10 million after using Twitter to promote a marketing company of which he was a shareholder. His endorsements company G Unit Brands Inc. controlled 12.9% of H&H Imports, a parent company of TV Goods, the firm responsible for marketing his range of headphones, Sleek by 50 Cent. Jackson bought stock in the company on November 30, 2010, a week after it offered buyers 180 million shares at $0.17 each. Jackson later made a stock recommendation on Twitter, causing its share price to rise from $0.04 to nearly $0.50, closing on Monday at $0.39. Jackson was later investigated by the [Securities and Exchange Commission](/wiki/Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "Securities and Exchange Commission") for breaching securities laws, as his tweet may have constituted [insider trading](/wiki/Insider_trading "Insider trading") with a [pump and dump](/wiki/Pump_and_dump "Pump and dump") investment strategy.[[174]](#cite_note-175)[[175]](#cite_note-176)[[176]](#cite_note-177)

In 2013, Jackson became a minority investor in Hang w/, a live video broadcasting mobile app used by dozens of celebrities to broadcast their daily activities and chat with fans. The app was downloaded more than 1 million times after launching in March 2013 and had more than 1 million users as of February 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=50_Cent&action=edit). Other minority celebrity investors were former [NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") player [Terrell Owens](/wiki/Terrell_Owens "Terrell Owens") and record producer [Timbaland](/wiki/Timbaland "Timbaland").[[177]](#cite_note-178)[[178]](#cite_note-179)[[179]](#cite_note-180)[[180]](#cite_note-181)

### Mining and heavy metals

In 2008, Jackson visited a platinum, palladium and iridium mine shaft in South Africa, and met with South African billionaire [Patrice Motsepe](/wiki/Patrice_Motsepe "Patrice Motsepe") in talks of purchasing an equity stake in the mine.[[133]](#cite_note-Kamelia_Angelova_and_Hilary_Lewis-134) After meeting with Motsepe, Jackson considered purchasing equity in the mine and launching his own line of 50 Cent branded platinum.[[181]](#cite_note-182)[[182]](#cite_note-183)

### Boxing promotion

On July 21, 2012, Jackson became a licensed [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") [promoter](/wiki/Promoter_(entertainment) "Promoter (entertainment)") when he formed his new company, TMT (The Money Team). Licensed to promote in New York, he was in the process of being licensed in [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada") (where most major fights are held in the U.S.). A former amateur boxer, Jackson signed gold medalist and former featherweight champion [Yuriorkis Gamboa](/wiki/Yuriorkis_Gamboa "Yuriorkis Gamboa") and middleweight Olympic medalist [Andre Dirrell](/wiki/Andre_Dirrell "Andre Dirrell").[[183]](#cite_note-184) On July 29, he and the boxer [Floyd Mayweather Jr.](/wiki/Floyd_Mayweather_Jr. "Floyd Mayweather Jr.") signed IBF featherweight champion [Billy Dib](/wiki/Billy_Dib "Billy Dib"). They unveiled plans to challenge the box-office dominance of [mixed martial arts](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts "Mixed martial arts") and change the landscape of boxing with TMT Promotions.[[184]](#cite_note-185) Boxer [Zab Judah](/wiki/Zab_Judah "Zab Judah") also expressed interest in making a deal with Jackson.[[185]](#cite_note-186) In December, Mayweather and Jackson parted company, with Jackson taking over the promotion company and founding SMS Promotions[[186]](#cite_note-187) with Gamboa, Dirrell, Dib, [James Kirkland](/wiki/James_Kirkland_(boxer) "James Kirkland (boxer)"), Luis Olivares, and Donte Strayhorn in his stable.

### Bankruptcy

On July 13, 2015, Jackson filed for [Chapter 11 bankruptcy](/wiki/Chapter_11,_Title_11,_United_States_Code "Chapter 11, Title 11, United States Code") protection in the U.S. Bankruptcy Court for the District of Connecticut with a debt of $32,509,549.91.[[187]](#cite_note-188)[[188]](#cite_note-189)[[189]](#cite_note-190) On July 17, the Court issued an order allowing a creditor to proceed with the punitive damages phase of a trial against Jackson in a New York state court in connection with the alleged release of a private video.[[190]](#cite_note-191) His assets were listed as between $10 million and $50 million in his bankruptcy petition, though he testified under oath that he was worth $4.4 million.[[191]](#cite_note-192) He said that he had between $10 million and $50 million in debt and the same amount in assets. Later in the week, Jackson's bankruptcy lawyers elucidated through the court documents that legal fees and judgments exceeding $20 million over the past year were the primary cause of the filing.[[192]](#cite_note-193)

His filings listed 32 entities in which he had a stake. The bankruptcy came days after a jury ordered him to pay $5 million to Rick Ross's ex-girlfriend Lastonia Leviston for invading her privacy by posting online a sex tape of her and another man.[[193]](#cite_note-194) In addition, Jackson lost a dispute over a failed business deal involving his Sleek headphones, where Jackson invested more than $2 million.[[194]](#cite_note-Gleason-195) An ex-partner accused Jackson of stealing the design of the "Sleek by 50" headphones, prompting a judge to award the partner more than $17.2 million.[[195]](#cite_note-196)[[196]](#cite_note-197) His Connecticut bankruptcy filing stated that he owned seven cars valued at more than $500,000, including a 2010 Rolls-Royce and a 1966 [Chevrolet](/wiki/Chevrolet "Chevrolet") Coupe.[[197]](#cite_note-198) His monthly expenses of $108,000 included $5,000 for gardening. He reported a monthly income of $185,000, mainly from royalties and his external businesses and investments. The court filing said he also owed money to his stylist, his barber, and his fitness coach.[[198]](#cite_note-199)[[199]](#cite_note-200)

Other details in the bankruptcy documents included information about two deals that sold the right to collect royalties of on-air play of his music. Half the rights to his portfolio were sold to the British independent music publishing company [Kobalt Music Group](/wiki/Kobalt_Music_Group "Kobalt Music Group") for $3 million and the other half for another $3 million, with the sales of his albums allowing Jackson to own the rights to the master recordings while paying only for distribution.[[200]](#cite_note-Stephanie_Gleason-201) Zeisler & Zeisler, a Bridgeport law firm, represented 50 Cent in the bankruptcy, which resulted in Jackson filing a $75 million lawsuit against his own lawyers.[[201]](#cite_note-202) He said his lawyers did a terrible job of representing him, specifically citing the fallout of his failed venture with Sleek Audio headphones. He accused Garvey Schubert Barer, a Wall Street law firm, of failing to "employ the requisite knowledge and skill necessary to confront the circumstances of the case".[[202]](#cite_note-203)[[203]](#cite_note-204)[[204]](#cite_note-205)[[205]](#cite_note-206)[[206]](#cite_note-207)[[194]](#cite_note-Gleason-195)[[207]](#cite_note-208)
