## Personal life

50 Cent has said his life philosophy since his shooting has been "When your time comes, you go. I think that comes from God."[[213]](#cite_note-214) Despite numerous songs that reference drug and alcohol use and his ownership of Branson Cognac, Jackson remains a [teetotaler](/wiki/Teetotaler "Teetotaler"), citing a bad experience with alcohol as his main reason.[[214]](#cite_note-215)[[215]](#cite_note-216)

*[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* noted Jackson's wealth in 2007, ranking him second behind [Jay-Z](/wiki/Jay-Z "Jay-Z") in the rap industry.[[216]](#cite_note-Hip-Hop_Cash_Kings-217) In 2003 he purchased, for $4.1 million, a [Farmington, Connecticut](/wiki/Farmington,_Connecticut "Farmington, Connecticut") mansion formerly owned by ex-boxer [Mike Tyson](/wiki/Mike_Tyson "Mike Tyson").[[217]](#cite_note-218) Jackson listed the mansion for sale in 2007 at $18.5 million to move closer to his son, who lived on [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island") at the time.[[218]](#cite_note-219) In 2019, the mansion sold for $2.9 million, with $1 million donated to tax-deducted charities.[[219]](#cite_note-220)[[220]](#cite_note-221)[[221]](#cite_note-222)

The mayor of [Bridgeport, Connecticut](/wiki/Bridgeport,_Connecticut "Bridgeport, Connecticut"), declared October 12, 2007 "50 Cent Curtis Jackson Day", honoring him with a proclamation and a [key to the city](/wiki/Key_to_the_City "Key to the City").[[222]](#cite_note-223) One of Jackson's New York homes, purchased in January 2007 for $2.4 million and the center of a lawsuit between Jackson and Shaniqua Tompkins, caught fire on May 31, 2008, while he was filming in Louisiana.[[223]](#cite_note-chicagotribune.com-224)

### Family and relationships

On October 13, 1997, Jackson's girlfriend, Shaniqua Tompkins, gave birth to son Marquise.[[224]](#cite_note-225) In 2009 Tompkins sued Jackson for $50 million, saying he promised to take care of her for life. The suit, with 15 [causes of action](/wiki/Cause_of_action "Cause of action"), was dismissed by a judge who called it "an unfortunate tale of a love relationship gone sour".[[225]](#cite_note-226)[[226]](#cite_note-227) The two have had a dispute for years and taken it to social media many times.[[227]](#cite_note-Bossip-228)[[228]](#cite_note-Feud-229)

Marquise's birth changed Jackson's outlook on life: "When my son came into my life, my priorities changed, because I wanted to have the relationship with him that I didn't have with my father."[[229]](#cite_note-230) He credited his son for inspiring his career and being the "motivation to go in a different direction".[[230]](#cite_note-231) Despite this, the two have endured a fractured relationship that began when Jackson and Tompkins separated in 2008.[[231]](#cite_note-232) Their feud has been taken to social media numerous times, including in 2020 when Jackson wrote that he "used to" love his son.[[232]](#cite_note-233) Jackson has a tattoo "Marquise" with an axe on his right biceps ("The axe is 'cause I'm a warrior. I don't want him to be one, though"),[[43]](#cite_note-Blender-44) and has "50", "Southside" and "Cold World" on his back: "I'm a product of that environment. It's on my back, though, so it's all behind me".[[43]](#cite_note-Blender-44)

In 2003, Jackson dated actress [Vivica A. Fox](/wiki/Vivica_A._Fox "Vivica A. Fox") for eight months.[[233]](#cite_note-234)

Jackson dated model Daphne Joy and had his second son, Sire Jackson, with her, on September 1, 2012.[[234]](#cite_note-235)[[235]](#cite_note-236)[[236]](#cite_note-237) At age two, Sire modeled for Kidz Safe, a headphone brand for kids, earning $700,000.[[237]](#cite_note-238)

In 2024, in an Instagram post promoting his [Cognac](/wiki/Cognac "Cognac") brand, Jackson wrote that he had been [practicing celibacy](/wiki/Sexual_abstinence "Sexual abstinence"): "My new idea is so big, I don't have time to be distracted I'm practicing abstinence, I have been meditating and focusing on my goals."[[238]](#cite_note-239) Jackson clarified his stance on *[The Late Show with Stephen Colbert](/wiki/The_Late_Show_with_Stephen_Colbert "The Late Show with Stephen Colbert")*, saying, "when you calm down you can focus....I've been good to me."[[239]](#cite_note-240) He also opened up about his decision to not marry, saying, "I'm safe. I'm not a happy hostage. I'm here. I'm free. I made some mistakes, just not that one....I want someone I can love in my life too, just not right now, I'm fine."[[240]](#cite_note-241)

### Political views

In 2005, Jackson supported President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") after rapper [Kanye West](/wiki/Kanye_West "Kanye West") criticized Bush for a slow response to the victims of [Hurricane Katrina](/wiki/Hurricane_Katrina "Hurricane Katrina").[[241]](#cite_note-242) Had his felony convictions not prevented him from voting, he said, he would have voted for Bush.[[242]](#cite_note-243) Jackson later said that Bush "has less compassion than the average human. By all means, I don't aspire to be like George Bush."[[243]](#cite_note-244) In September 2007, he told *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* that although he would not endorse a candidate in 2008, he "liked [Hillary [Clinton]](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton")".[[244]](#cite_note-245)

Six months later, Jackson told [MTV News](/wiki/MTV_News "MTV News") that he had switched his support to [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") after hearing him speak but had lost interest in politics.[[245]](#cite_note-246)[[246]](#cite_note-247) Asked his opinion of Obama's 2012 endorsement of [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage"), Jackson said, "I'm for it ... I've encouraged same-sex activities. I've engaged in fetish areas a couple times."[[247]](#cite_note-248) He had been criticized for anti-gay comments in the past.[[248]](#cite_note-glaad-249)[[249]](#cite_note-250)[[250]](#cite_note-251)

Jackson endorsed Democratic nominee [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton") in the run-up to the 2016 U.S. presidential election.[[251]](#cite_note-252) He rejected an offer of $500,000 from the [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") campaign to make an appearance on Trump's behalf.[[252]](#cite_note-253) In 2020 he endorsed Trump due to his dislike of [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden")'s tax plans.[[253]](#cite_note-254) A week later, he retracted his endorsement, writing on Twitter, "Fuck Donald Trump, I never liked him",[[254]](#cite_note-255) and endorsed Biden.[[255]](#cite_note-256)

In June 2025, Jackson posted on Instagram a video of [New York City mayoral candidate](/wiki/2025_New_York_City_mayoral_election "2025 New York City mayoral election") [Zohran Mamdani](/wiki/Zohran_Mamdani "Zohran Mamdani") explaining his tax policy, writing: "I'm not feeling this plan no. I will give him $258,750 and a first class one way ticket away from NY. I'm telling Trump what he said too!"[[256]](#cite_note-257)

## Legal issues

On June 29, 1994, Jackson was arrested for selling four vials of [cocaine](/wiki/Cocaine "Cocaine") to an undercover police officer. He was arrested again three weeks later, when police searched his home and found [heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin"), ten ounces of [crack cocaine](/wiki/Crack_cocaine "Crack cocaine") and a [starter's pistol](/wiki/Starting_pistol "Starting pistol"). Although Jackson was sentenced to three to nine years in prison, he served six months in a [boot camp](/wiki/Boot_camp_(correctional) "Boot camp (correctional)") (where he earned his high-school equivalency diploma). According to him, he did not use cocaine.[[17]](#cite_note-OMM-18)[[24]](#cite_note-rapnewsdirect.com-25)[[257]](#cite_note-258)

Jackson and four members of his entourage were arrested shortly before 2 a.m. on December 31, 2002, when police found a .25-caliber handgun and a .45-caliber pistol in a parked car (which they searched due to its tinted windows) outside a Manhattan nightclub. The rapper was charged with two counts of criminal possession of a weapon.[[258]](#cite_note-259)

Jackson was sentenced to two years' [probation](/wiki/Probation "Probation") on July 22, 2005, for a May 2004 incident, when he was charged with three counts of [assault](/wiki/Assault "Assault") and [battery](/wiki/Battery_(crime) "Battery (crime)") after jumping into an audience when he was hit by a water bottle.

### Lawsuits

#### Use of image

Jackson filed a lawsuit against an advertising company, Traffix of [Pearl River, New York](/wiki/Pearl_River,_New_York "Pearl River, New York"), on July 21, 2007, for using his image in a promotion he said threatened his safety. He was alerted by a staff member to an Internet advertisement on a [Myspace](/wiki/Myspace "Myspace") page. According to court documents, the advertisement had a cartoon image of the rapper with "Shoot the rapper and you will win $5000 or five ring tones guaranteed". Although the ad did not use his name, the image allegedly resembled him and suggested that he endorsed the product. The lawsuit, calling the ad a "vile, tasteless and despicable" use of Jackson's image which "quite literally call[ed] for violence against him", sought unspecified [punitive damages](/wiki/Punitive_damages "Punitive damages") and a permanent injunction against the use of his image without permission.[[259]](#cite_note-260)[[260]](#cite_note-261)

#### Use of name

In 2008, Jackson sued [Taco Bell](/wiki/Taco_Bell "Taco Bell") over an ad campaign that used his name without permission, in which it invited him to change his name for one day from 50 Cent to 79 Cent, 89 Cent, or 99 Cent, in line with pricing for some of its items, and they would donate $10,000 to the charity of his choice. The case was settled out of court in his favor in November 2009.[[261]](#cite_note-262)[[262]](#cite_note-263)[[263]](#cite_note-264)

#### Janitor incident

While walking through [Cincinnati/Northern Kentucky International Airport](/wiki/Cincinnati/Northern_Kentucky_International_Airport "Cincinnati/Northern Kentucky International Airport") in May 2016, Jackson harassed and insulted a janitor at the airport, accusing him of being [under the influence](/wiki/Altered_state_of_consciousness "Altered state of consciousness"). The janitor was a hearing-impaired, [autistic](/wiki/Autistic "Autistic") teenager named Andrew Farrell. The parents of the janitor had seen the viral video as disrespect and wanted to sue Jackson for his action against their child. The lawsuit was originally over one million dollars, but the parents settled for a $100,000 donation to [Autism Speaks](/wiki/Autism_Speaks "Autism Speaks") and his apology.[[264]](#cite_note-265)[[265]](#cite_note-266)[[266]](#cite_note-267)[[267]](#cite_note-268)[[268]](#cite_note-269)

#### Bamba sample

In 2016, a judge declared that Brandon Parrott gave Dr. Dre and 50 Cent the rights to "Bamba" for the song "[P.I.M.P.](/wiki/P.I.M.P. "P.I.M.P.")"[[269]](#cite_note-270)

### Other civil and criminal matters

One of his New York homes, purchased for $2.4 million in January 2007 and the center of a lawsuit between Jackson and Shaniqua Tompkins, caught fire on May 30, 2008, while he was filming in Louisiana.[[223]](#cite_note-chicagotribune.com-224) On August 5, 2013, Jackson pleaded not guilty to one count of domestic violence and four counts of vandalism in a Los Angeles County court. If convicted of all charges, he faced up to five years in prison and a $46,000 fine. Model-actress Daphne Joy accused Jackson of kicking her and ransacking her bedroom during an argument at her condominium in the Toluca Lake neighborhood of Los Angeles on June 23. He allegedly caused $7,100 in property damage, leaving the scene before police arrived.[[270]](#cite_note-271)

Judge Ann Nevins has ordered Jackson back to court because of Instagram messages he made over several months.[[271]](#cite_note-272) She said Jackson was not fully clear about his funds and indicated posts of the rapper showing stacks of his money. In March 2016, Jackson claimed that he would no longer use [Instagram](/wiki/Instagram "Instagram"), electing instead to have his profile page operated by someone else.[[272]](#cite_note-273)

In June 2018, after former linebacker and actor [Terry Crews](/wiki/Terry_Crews "Terry Crews") gave a speech before the [United States Senate Committee on the Judiciary](/wiki/United_States_Senate_Committee_on_the_Judiciary "United States Senate Committee on the Judiciary") in which he described being groped by a Hollywood agent in 2016, Jackson posted a photo on Instagram of a shirtless muscular Crews with the caption: "I got raped. My wife just watched."[[273]](#cite_note-274)[[274]](#cite_note-275)[[275]](#cite_note-276) After receiving backlash, Jackson deleted the post and wrote on Twitter: "People are so sensitive."[[276]](#cite_note-277)[[277]](#cite_note-278) In a July 2018 appearance on *[The View](/wiki/The_View "The View")*, Jackson stated that he would "never make fun of any sexual assault victim", adding: "I wasn't looking at Terry Crews that way, at that point. I'm looking at the [Hulk](/wiki/Hulk "Hulk"). The guy is this big, that's being taken advantage of."[[278]](#cite_note-279)

In 2020, Jackson was a subject of controversy for his involvement in a viral video of him giving money to a [Burger King](/wiki/Burger_King "Burger King") restaurant in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") on behalf of a local scammer who was later arrested and charged for [Bitcoin](/wiki/Bitcoin "Bitcoin") scamming and for assaulting and kidnapping his victims on April 24, 2021.[[279]](#cite_note-280)[[280]](#cite_note-281)[[281]](#cite_note-282)

## Feuds

### Ja Rule

Before he signed with Interscope Records, Jackson engaged in a public dispute with rapper [Ja Rule](/wiki/Ja_Rule "Ja Rule") and his label, [Murder Inc. Records](/wiki/Murder_Inc._Records "Murder Inc. Records"), saying that a friend robbed jewelry from Ja Rule and the latter accused him of orchestrating the robbery.[[282]](#cite_note-Playboy-283) Ja Rule said that the conflict stemmed from a Queens video shoot, when Jackson did not like seeing him "getting so much love" from the neighborhood.[[283]](#cite_note-284) At [The Hit Factory](/wiki/The_Hit_Factory "The Hit Factory") in New York in March 2000, Jackson had an altercation with Murder Inc. associates and received three stitches for a stab wound.[[282]](#cite_note-Playboy-283)[[284]](#cite_note-285) Rapper Black Child claimed responsibility for the stabbing, saying that he acted in self-defense when he thought someone reached for a gun.[[285]](#cite_note-286)

An [affidavit](/wiki/Affidavit "Affidavit") by an [Internal Revenue Service](/wiki/Internal_Revenue_Service "Internal Revenue Service") (IRS) agent suggested ties between Murder Inc. and [Kenneth "Supreme" McGriff](/wiki/Kenneth_McGriff "Kenneth McGriff"), a New York drug lord suspected of involvement in the murder of [Jam Master Jay](/wiki/Jam_Master_Jay "Jam Master Jay") and Jackson's shooting. An excerpt read:

> The investigation has uncovered a conspiracy involving McGriff and others to murder a rap artist who has released songs containing lyrics regarding McGriff's criminal activities. The rap artist was shot in 2000, survived and thereafter refused to cooperate with law enforcement regarding the shooting. Messages transmitted over the Murder Inc. pager indicate that McGriff is involved in an ongoing plot to kill this rap artist, and that he communicates with Murder Inc. employees concerning the target.[[42]](#cite_note-TSG-43)

The end of the Jackson-Ja Rule feud was confirmed in May 2011. According to Ja Rule, "I'm cool. We ain't beefing no more. We'll never collaborate. That's just what it is. You don't have to be at war with somebody, but it's also kind of like U.S. and another country that they may not get along with. We don't gotta go to war, but we're not friends either. But we can coincide inside of a world. He's doing him, and he's not thinking about me, and I'm doing me and I'm not thinking about him."[[286]](#cite_note-287)

On August 7, 2015, the feud between the two rappers later reignited when Ja Rule gave a feedback to a social follower via Twitter over a similar feud between [Meek Mill](/wiki/Meek_Mill "Meek Mill") and [Drake](/wiki/Drake_(rapper) "Drake (rapper)"). Enraged, Jackson later responded with photos and comments via Instagram, only siding with Drake.[[287]](#cite_note-288) The feud resurfaced three years later on January 19, 2018, when Ja Rule took to Twitter, calling out 50 Cent on social media.[[288]](#cite_note-289)

In October 2018, Jackson pranked Ja Rule by purchasing 200 discounted front-row tickets for an upcoming show of his in [Arlington, Texas](/wiki/Arlington,_Texas "Arlington, Texas") for a total of US$3,000 via [Groupon](/wiki/Groupon "Groupon"), with Jackson later confirming on [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media") that he bought the tickets with the express intention of forcing Ja Rule to perform in front of rows of empty seats.[[289]](#cite_note-The_Times-290)

### Jay-Z and Beyoncé

In 1999, with the release of the single "[How to Rob](/wiki/How_to_Rob "How to Rob")", 50 Cent attacked his rap peers, including Jay-Z, who responded to the provocation with the single "It's Hot (Some Like It Hot)" in which he raps "Go against Jigga yo ass is dense/I'm about a dollar – what the f\*ck is 50 Cents?".[[290]](#cite_note-291)

In a 2009 interview with *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")*, 50 Cent accused Jay-Z and his record company [Roc-A-Fella Records](/wiki/Roc-A-Fella_Records "Roc-A-Fella Records") of being unfair to [Beanie Sigel](/wiki/Beanie_Sigel "Beanie Sigel") because "[Jay-Z] didn't have their interests in mind, he was just doing what he had to do for himself." The rapper also stated that "I don't know him personally, [...] I tried to collaborate with him on the [Freeway](/wiki/Freeway_Ricky_Ross "Freeway Ricky Ross") project ... What I did was Freeway went out and found his publishing deal, and we started the album. [...] when it came time to put the record out, he didn't want to shoot his video. He has a [god complex](/wiki/God_complex "God complex")."[[291]](#cite_note-292) 50 Cent also accused Jay-Z of changing in attitude and image since marrying [Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9 "Beyoncé").

In 2017, 50 Cent described Jay-Z's thirteenth [studio album](/wiki/Album "Album") *[4:44](/wiki/4:44 "4:44")* as "golf course music" in an [Instagram](/wiki/Instagram "Instagram") post, elaborating: "I listened to Jay sh\*t, that *4:44*. [...] [It] was too smart. I felt like I was supposed to be wearing like glasses and shit and like a tie, a fucking sweater around my waist. It was like [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League"). They don't wanna hear that shit. [...] They just wanna have a good time. You know what I'm saying? You can't be the best rapper at 47 because the new n\*\*\*\*s is here. They coming with new [music] going on."[[292]](#cite_note-293) In an interview on the [*Conan Show*](/wiki/Conan_(talk_show) "Conan (talk show)"), 50 Cent spoke about the album, saying: "Hip-hop culture's connected to youth culture. The kids gonna bring new innovative stuff. He just had the maturity bleed off into the material. [...] But the kids, I don't see them actually listening to it".[[293]](#cite_note-294)[[294]](#cite_note-295)

In an interview in 2022, 50 Cent reported that in a meeting between him and the couple in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), the two rappers were having a heated argument. Beyoncé, seeing them, jumped from a ledge to come to her husband's defense. 50 Cent compared the singer to her sister [Solange](/wiki/Solange_Knowles "Solange Knowles") when she hit Jay-Z in the elevator, describing them as "two gangstas".[[295]](#cite_note-296)[[296]](#cite_note-297)

In an interview for [*XXL Mag*](/wiki/XXL_(magazine) "XXL (magazine)") in 2023, 50 Cent claimed that Jay-Z's [Grammy](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards") wins were due to his marriage to Beyoncé: "Even Jay-Z's career, you can look at that and say the association to Beyoncé is when he started to receive the 16, 17 Grammys, since he's been with Beyoncé. And you go, prior to that [he had] one. That came with the association."[[297]](#cite_note-298) 50 Cent also pointed out that the couple's eldest child [Blue Ivy Carter](/wiki/Blue_Ivy_Carter "Blue Ivy Carter") won a Grammy even though she does not sing or rap, accusing award ceremonies of giving accolades to the couple just to ensure their attendance during the show.[[298]](#cite_note-299)[[299]](#cite_note-300) The rapper also reported that his non-win at the Grammys was due to his music being "a little bit abrasive to conservatives; [...] It didn't matter to me in that time period. Don't give me the trophy. They didn't give me Best New Artist and I sold 13 million records", while the Carter-Knowles family is more politically agreeable to the music industry.[[300]](#cite_note-301)

### Fat Joe

In connection to his feud with Ja Rule, 50 Cent also took aim at fellow New York rapper [Fat Joe](/wiki/Fat_Joe "Fat Joe"), a high-profile collaborator of Ja Rule's.[[301]](#cite_note-302)[[302]](#cite_note-303) The feud took aim at Fat Joe following his 2002 track "[What's Luv?](/wiki/What%27s_Luv%3F "What's Luv?")" featuring Ja Rule and [Ashanti](/wiki/Ashanti_(singer) "Ashanti (singer)"). Fat Joe had also continued his collaboration with [Murder Inc.](/wiki/Murder_Inc._(rap_group) "Murder Inc. (rap group)") on his 2004 track "New York" featuring [Jadakiss](/wiki/Jadakiss "Jadakiss"), prompting a diss track aimed at Fat Joe from 50 Cent on his 2005 album *[The Massacre](/wiki/The_Massacre "The Massacre")* entitled "[Piggy Bank](/wiki/Piggy_Bank_(song) "Piggy Bank (song)")". Fat Joe later responded with the track "My Fofo" on his album *[All or Nothing](/wiki/All_or_Nothing_(Fat_Joe_album) "All or Nothing (Fat Joe album)")* released later that same year. Tensions boiled over during the [2005 MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/2005_MTV_Video_Music_Awards "2005 MTV Video Music Awards") when Fat Joe presented the award for best Hip Hop Video, in which he told the audience "I feel so safe tonight with all this police protection courtesy of [G-Unit](/wiki/G-Unit "G-Unit")". 50 Cent later retaliated with a profanity-laden diatribe following his performance of the song "[So Seductive](/wiki/So_Seductive "So Seductive")" with fellow G-Unit artist [Tony Yayo](/wiki/Tony_Yayo "Tony Yayo"), exclaiming "Fat Joe's a pussy man! [...] Pussy Boy, Nigga What?!". The feud later affected Fat Joe financially as he sought to secure a $20 million contract endorsing [Air Jordan](/wiki/Air_Jordan "Air Jordan") sneakers in early 2005, [Michael Jordan](/wiki/Michael_Jordan "Michael Jordan") himself later cancelled the contract following the VMA incident as he was in fear of conflict with 50 Cent or any other affiliates.[[303]](#cite_note-304) Following this; little action was taken on either side as the feud seemed to calm down by 2011. Murder Inc. executive [Irv Gotti](/wiki/Irv_Gotti "Irv Gotti") later expressed anger at Fat Joe following his proposal to quell their longstanding feud with G-Unit in 2010.[[304]](#cite_note-305) In 2012, 50 Cent approached Fat Joe in talks of ending the feud following the two agreeing to perform at a memorial ceremony for then-recently deceased music executive [Chris Lighty](/wiki/Chris_Lighty "Chris Lighty").[[305]](#cite_note-306) Despite the prior history between the two, 50 Cent and Fat Joe became close friends and business associates shortly thereafter despite Fat Joe's lengthy connections with Ja Rule.[[306]](#cite_note-307)[[307]](#cite_note-308)

### The Game

Although Jackson was close to [The Game](/wiki/The_Game_(rapper) "The Game (rapper)") before the latter released his debut album, *The Documentary*, they grew apart. After *The Documentary*'s release, Jackson felt that The Game was disloyal for saying that he did not want to participate in G-Unit's feuds with other rappers (such as [Nas](/wiki/Nas "Nas"), [Jadakiss](/wiki/Jadakiss "Jadakiss") and [Fat Joe](/wiki/Fat_Joe "Fat Joe")) and his desire to work with artists with which G-Unit was feuding. He said that he wrote six songs for the album and did not receive proper credit, which The Game denied.[[308]](#cite_note-309)

[![Black-and-white closeup of 50 Cent singing onstage](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/50_cent_in_concert.jpg/250px-50_cent_in_concert.jpg)](/wiki/File:50_cent_in_concert.jpg)

50 Cent at a 2007 concert

Jackson later dismissed The Game from G-Unit on [Hot 97](/wiki/WQHT "WQHT"). After the announcement, The Game (a guest earlier in the evening) tried to enter the building with his entourage. After they were denied entry, one of his associates was shot in the leg in a confrontation with a group of men leaving the building.[[309]](#cite_note-310)[[310]](#cite_note-311) When the situation escalated, the rappers held a joint press conference announcing their reconciliation,[[311]](#cite_note-312) and fans were uncertain if the rappers had staged a publicity stunt to boost sales of their recently released albums.[[312]](#cite_note-313) After the situation cooled,[[313]](#cite_note-314) G-Unit criticized The Game's [street credibility](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/street_credibility "wikt:street credibility") and announced that they would not appear on his albums. During a [Summer Jam](/wiki/Summer_Jam_(festival) "Summer Jam (festival)") performance The Game announced a [boycott](/wiki/Boycott "Boycott") of G-Unit, which he called "G-Unot".[[314]](#cite_note-315)

After the Summer Jam performance The Game recorded "300 Bars and Runnin'", an extended "diss" of G-Unit and Roc-A-Fella Records, for the mixtape *You Know What It Is Vol. 3*. Jackson responded with his "[Piggy Bank](/wiki/Piggy_Bank_(song) "Piggy Bank (song)")" music video, with The Game as [Mr. Potato Head](/wiki/Mr._Potato_Head "Mr. Potato Head") and parodies of other rivals.[[315]](#cite_note-316) They have continued attacking each other, with The Game releasing two more mixtapes: *Ghost Unit* and a mixtape-DVD, *[Stop Snitchin, Stop Lyin](/wiki/Stop_Snitchin,_Stop_Lyin "Stop Snitchin, Stop Lyin")*. Jackson superimposed The Game's head on the body of a male stripper for the cover of the *Hate It or Love It (G-Unit Radio Part 21)* mixtape in response to The Game's pictures of G-Unit dressed as the [Village People](/wiki/Village_People "Village People").[[316]](#cite_note-317) The Game, under contract to Aftermath Entertainment, signed with [Geffen Records](/wiki/Geffen_Records "Geffen Records") to terminate his contractual obligations with G-Unit (although it is claimed that Jackson pressured Dr. Dre to fire him).[[317]](#cite_note-318) G-Unit member [Spider Loc](/wiki/Spider_Loc "Spider Loc") has insulted The Game in songs, and the latter released "240 Bars (Spider Joke)" and "100 Bars (The Funeral)" attacking G-Unit and Loc. Jackson's response was "Not Rich, Still Lyin'", mocking The Game.[[318]](#cite_note-240Bars-319) Lloyd Banks replied to the Game on a *[Rap City](/wiki/Rap_City_(BET_program) "Rap City (BET program)")* freestyle-booth segment, followed by a Game "diss" song ("SoundScan") ridiculing the 13-position drop of Banks' album *Rotten Apple* on the *Billboard* 200 chart and its disappointing second-week sales. Banks replied on his mixtape *Mo' Money In The Bank Pt. 5: Gang Green Season Continues* with "Showtime (The Game's Over)", said that Jackson wrote half of *The Documentary* and ridiculed The Game's suicidal thoughts.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In October 2006, The Game made a peace overture (which was not immediately answered) to Jackson,[[319]](#cite_note-320) but two days later he said on [Power 106](/wiki/KPWR "KPWR") that the peace offer was valid for only one day.[[320]](#cite_note-321) In several songs on *Doctor's Advocate*, he implied that the feud was over. He said in July 2009 that the feud had ended with help from [Michael Jackson](/wiki/Michael_Jackson "Michael Jackson") and [Sean Combs](/wiki/Sean_Combs "Sean Combs"),[[321]](#cite_note-322) and apologized for his actions.[[322]](#cite_note-323) According to [Tony Yayo](/wiki/Tony_Yayo "Tony Yayo"), neither Jackson nor G-Unit accepted his apology[[323]](#cite_note-324) and The Game has resumed his calls for a "G-Unot" boycott at concerts. Jackson released "So Disrespectful" on *[Before I Self Destruct](/wiki/Before_I_Self_Destruct "Before I Self Destruct")*, targeting Jay-Z, The Game and [Young Buck](/wiki/Young_Buck "Young Buck").[[324]](#cite_note-325) The Game responded with "Shake", poking fun at the music video for Jackson's "[Candy Shop](/wiki/Candy_Shop "Candy Shop")".

On January 2, 2015, The Game claimed that he and 50 were "sworn enemies", promising never to reconcile with him anymore,[[325]](#cite_note-326) but on August 1, 2016, they ended their twelve-year feud when the two were in the Ace of Diamonds strip club and The Game said "I love 50, man. What happened, that shit was 12 years ago."[[326]](#cite_note-327)

In January 2022, the feud reignited after 50 critiqued Game's *Drink Champs* interview with [N.O.R.E.](/wiki/N.O.R.E. "N.O.R.E."), where he claimed that 50's former competitor Kanye West did "more for me in two weeks than [Dr.] Dre did for me throughout my entire career".[[327]](#cite_note-328) The Game responded, commenting that he enclosed the entirety of G-Unit as a group and clothing brand "in a casket", also expressing his likeliness in the [*Power*](/wiki/Power_(TV_series) "Power (TV series)") television trilogy, but warning 50 to "leave [the past] alone or else... I'm outside #Numinati".[[328]](#cite_note-329) Then, two months later, in March, 50 Cent published a video via Instagram of Game being shunned by former Interscope Records CEO [Jimmy Iovine](/wiki/Jimmy_Iovine "Jimmy Iovine") at a basketball game, poking fun of it while also commenting "50 wrote ya hits". The Game once again flamed 50 after the claims were brought back up and also bragged to "get [50's] girlfriend out of my DM's".[[329]](#cite_note-330)[[330]](#cite_note-331) This was believed to have been a consequential result of Game claiming on *Drink Champs* to be "the best and a better rapper" than Eminem, with whom 50 still remains close friends.[[331]](#cite_note-332)[[332]](#cite_note-333) Additionally, Game's manager, Wack 100, has subliminally called out or questioned 50 Cent's credibility, over the rapper's surprise appearance at the [Super Bowl LVI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVI "Super Bowl LVI") halftime show and ghostwriting allegations.[[333]](#cite_note-334)[[334]](#cite_note-335)[[335]](#cite_note-336)[[336]](#cite_note-337)

### Cam'ron

Jackson's issues with former [The Diplomats](/wiki/The_Diplomats "The Diplomats") member [Cam'ron](/wiki/Cam%27ron "Cam'ron") began in 2007, when they had a live argument on *The [Angie Martinez](/wiki/Angie_Martinez "Angie Martinez") Show* on [Hot 97](/wiki/WQHT "WQHT") radio. Jackson commented that he felt that the music division of [Koch Entertainment](/wiki/Koch_Entertainment "Koch Entertainment") (known today as [MNRK Music Group](/wiki/MNRK_Music_Group "MNRK Music Group")) was a "graveyard", meaning major record labels would not work with their artists.[[337]](#cite_note-NYT-338) Cam'ron then ridiculed the record sales of G-Unit members Lloyd Banks and [Mobb Deep](/wiki/Mobb_Deep "Mobb Deep") by pointing out that [Dipset](/wiki/The_Diplomats "The Diplomats") member [Jim Jones](/wiki/Jim_Jones_(rapper) "Jim Jones (rapper)") outsold both of their albums despite not being signed to a major label, and also went on to clarify that his group, The Diplomats, had a distribution deal from several labels.[[337]](#cite_note-NYT-338) Both rappers released diss songs with videos on [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube"). Jackson released "Funeral Music", and suggested in the song that Cam'ron is no longer able to lead The Diplomats and that Jim Jones should take his place. Cam'ron responded with "Curtis" and "Curtis Pt. II", in which he makes fun of not only Jackson's first name, but also his appearance, calling him "a gorilla, with rabbit teeth". Jackson responded by releasing "[Hold On](/wiki/Hold_On_(Young_Buck_song) "Hold On (Young Buck song)")" with [Young Buck](/wiki/Young_Buck "Young Buck"). Since 2009, the feud slowly died down, and they eventually reconciled in 2016.[[338]](#cite_note-339)

### Rick Ross

Although [Rick Ross](/wiki/Rick_Ross "Rick Ross") began a feud with Jackson over an alleged incident at the [2008 BET Hip Hop Awards](/wiki/2008_BET_Hip_Hop_Awards "2008 BET Hip Hop Awards"), Jackson told news sources he did not remember seeing Ross there.[[339]](#cite_note-340) Later that month Ross' "[Mafia Music](/wiki/Deeper_Than_Rap "Deeper Than Rap")" was leaked on the Internet, with lyrics apparently disparaging Jackson. Several days later, Jackson released "Officer Ricky (Go Head, Try Me)" in response to "Mafia Music". The following day, Ross appeared on Shade 45 (Eminem's Sirius channel) and told Jackson to come up with something better in 24 hours.

Before leaving for Venezuela, Jackson uploaded a video ("Warning Shot") and the first of a series of "Officer Ricky" cartoons. In early February he uploaded a YouTube video in which he interviewed "Tia", the mother of one of Ross' children; according to her, Ross is in reality a [correctional officer](/wiki/Prison_officer "Prison officer").[[340]](#cite_note-341) On February 5, 2009, The Game phoned Seattle radio station [KUBE](/wiki/KUBE_(FM) "KUBE (FM)"). Asked about the dispute between Jackson and Ross, he sided with Jackson and offered to mediate: "Rick Ross, holla at your boy, man" and "50 eating you, boy."[[341]](#cite_note-342)

On his album *[Deeper Than Rap](/wiki/Deeper_Than_Rap "Deeper Than Rap")*, Ross refers to Jackson in "In Cold Blood" and Jackson's mock funeral is part of the song's video. When the song was released, Ross said that he ended Jackson's career.[[342]](#cite_note-343) "Rick Ross is Albert From [CB4](/wiki/CB4 "CB4"). You ever seen the movie? He's Albert," Jackson replied in an interview. "It never gets worse than this. You get a guy that was a correctional officer come out and base his entire career on writing material from a drug dealer's perspective such as ["Freeway" Ricky Ross](/wiki/%22Freeway%22_Rick_Ross "\"Freeway\" Rick Ross")."[[343]](#cite_note-344) Their feud rekindled at the 2012 BET Hip Hop Awards, where Jackson and G-Unit members Kidd Kidd, Mike Knox, Tony Yayo were seen on video attacking [Gunplay](/wiki/Gunplay_(rapper) "Gunplay (rapper)") (a member of Ross' Maybach Music Group). Gunplay's Maybach Music diamond necklace was stolen during the brawl, and several days later Jackson appeared at a Washington, D.C. bowling alley wearing Gunplay's chain.[[344]](#cite_note-345) On January 30, 2013, Jackson tweeted that Ross' attempted drive-by shooting on his birthday three days earlier was "staged".[[345]](#cite_note-346)

### Floyd Mayweather Jr.

Jackson and [Floyd Mayweather Jr.](/wiki/Floyd_Mayweather_Jr. "Floyd Mayweather Jr.") were close friends in the early 2000s, sharing key personal milestones including Mayweather's birthday party in Las Vegas in 2007. In 2012, he and Jackson co-founded the company "The Money Team" also known as “TMT”, which signs up-and-coming boxers. On December 21, 2011, Mayweather was sentenced to 90 days in county jail for domestic violence and battery. He started serving his sentence on June 1, leaving Jackson to run the promotion company. Mayweather was released two months later on August 3. Jackson then claimed Mayweather owed him $1 million for work done for TMT during Mayweather's jail sentence. This caused a public dispute in which the two traded insults via online videos.

Jackson then claimed Mayweather couldn't read and challenged him to read a full Harry Potter book in exchange for a donation of $750,000 to a charity of Mayweather's choosing. Mayweather rejected the challenge and insulted Jackson's strained relationship with his son, offering Jackson $1 million if he could post a video of his son saying "I love you".[[346]](#cite_note-347) They continued to trade insults until in 2022, when Jackson & Mayweather reconciled in Las Vegas.

### Sean Combs

Jackson's feud with [Sean "Diddy" Combs](/wiki/Sean_%22Diddy%22_Combs "Sean \"Diddy\" Combs") began in 2006, when Jackson accused Combs of complicity in [Biggie's](/wiki/Notorious_BIG "Notorious BIG") murder in his diss track "The Bomb". Over the following decades, Jackson publicly criticized Combs several times, including insulting his rival vodka brand [Cîroc](/wiki/C%C3%AEroc "Cîroc") (Jackson endorsed [Effen](/wiki/Effen "Effen")) and complaining that Combs made him uncomfortable when he invited him to go shopping.[[347]](#cite_note-auto-348)

In 2023, when Combs' ex-girlfriend [Cassie Ventura](/wiki/Cassie_Ventura "Cassie Ventura") accused him of domestic abuse and rape, Jackson again taunted Combs, jokingly offering to take his place in endorsement deals that had abandoned Combs, and posting about the police raid on Combs' home. When CNN released video corroborating Cassie's allegation that Combs punched her, Jackson told the [Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/Hollywood_Reporter "Hollywood Reporter") "When someone watches that, if they have a daughter and they can imagine her being under those circumstances, that shit is crazy. Like, they let him get away with it."[[347]](#cite_note-auto-348)

Jackson has also stated he never attended any of [the parties Combs hosted](/wiki/Diddy_parties "Diddy parties") at which the latter was accused of engaging in sex trafficking, saying the parties had an "uncomfortable energy".

In 2024, Jackson announced he was executive producing a documentary about Combs sexual abuse and sex trafficking case, *[Sean Combs: The Reckoning](/wiki/Sean_Combs:_The_Reckoning "Sean Combs: The Reckoning")*, released on Netflix on December 2, 2025.[[348]](#cite_note-349)
